Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I couldn't afford to attend classes regularly because I work part-time to support myself, so I had to be strategic about when I studied. I'd usually block out chunks of time on weekends and late evenings when I wasn't working, and I'd go really hard during those sessions. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create diagrams and mind maps from the materials to maximize what I could absorb in limited time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on my work schedule and when assignments were due. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was pulling extra shifts, then I'd cram intensively when deadlines approached. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work. I learned to be flexible because my life doesn't really allow for a perfect, consistent routine.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new assignment postings or downloading materials I needed for my offline study sessions. Sometimes I'd log in because a peer messaged me about group work or to clarify something. I didn't browse around much—I went in with a purpose and got out because my time was limited.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a daily routine for me, but I did develop a pattern of logging in at the start of each week to see what was new. It became kind of automatic to check on Sunday nights before my busy week started. That way I could plan what to download and study during whatever free moments I had.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me because I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them once, sometimes at 1.5x speed, take screenshots of key diagrams, and then study from those offline. The peer interaction features were also helpful—connecting with other students helped me fill gaps when I missed classes. Those 37 interactions I had with peers honestly saved me multiple times.
Q6: The discussion forums—I never posted anything there. It felt like a time commitment I couldn't justify when I had limited hours to study. I also didn't use the extra reading materials much because I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. With low resources, I had to be ruthless about what got my attention.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or assignments. I learned early on to identify what was high-yield versus what was supplementary. If I could see how something would help me perform better on assessments, I was all in. Otherwise, I had to let it go—there's only so many hours in a day when you're working.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones got done no matter what because they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd only engage if I genuinely had extra time, which was rare. I don't resent them being there, but I had to triage constantly.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, save video screenshots, and work from printed notes during my bus commute to work or during breaks. I also used free resources I found online—YouTube videos explaining concepts, that kind of thing. The LMS was more like my supply depot than my actual study space.
Q10: There were countless hours where I was studying from downloaded materials or my handwritten notes that the system would never capture. I'd sit in the break room at work reviewing diagrams I'd drawn, or I'd quiz myself mentally while doing repetitive tasks. My exam score reflects learning that happened completely outside the LMS.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or take detailed screenshots, and then review those materials dozens of times offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab everything I needed in one session and then work independently. It was more efficient for my situation anyway.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when work got busier and I was exhausted. I remember feeling surprised at how quickly I'd fallen behind on attendance. The stress built up, but I kept telling myself I'd figure it out—and I did. My resilience kicked in when things got tough.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor. After a long shift, the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and watch lectures. Competing responsibilities—work, helping out at home—constantly pulled me away. I'd sometimes log in intending to study but end up just checking what was due and logging off because I was too drained.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. I'd be physically present but mentally checked out from exhaustion. Those sessions felt wasted, but I learned to recognize when I was too tired and just stop instead of pretending to study.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-priority compared to assignments and exam prep. I knew participation mattered, but I had to make hard choices. Sometimes I'd avoid starting an assignment because I was anxious about not having enough time to do it well, but then I'd power through anyway.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd spike in engagement right before things were due—that's just how I had to operate. The deadline pressure actually helped me focus intensely. Without them, I might have spread myself too thin trying to do everything.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was specific and showed me where to improve. After getting good marks on exams, I felt validated that my unconventional approach was working. It encouraged me to trust my methods even when my attendance and assignment completion looked concerning on paper.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty accommodating because materials were available to access anytime. That flexibility was essential for someone with my schedule. However, the emphasis on regular attendance didn't fit my reality, so I had to compensate in other ways.
Q19: Having downloadable resources made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or consistent weekly involvement—my life just doesn't work that way. More asynchronous options would have helped students like me even more.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to translate that into completed work. They could be dealing with personal issues, perfectionism, or just not knowing how to start. High activity might mean they're trying hard but hitting barriers that aren't visible to instructors.
Q21: That's literally my experience. My LMS activity looks minimal, but I performed really well because I studied efficiently offline. The system only captures one slice of learning behavior. Some of us have developed strategies that work outside the traditional online engagement model.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and submission timing pretty accurately. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and all the learning that happens through peer conversations outside the platform. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material and trying to understand it deeply, not just going through motions. It's about mental investment, making connections, and caring about actually learning—not just logging hours. For me, engagement happens in my head, not necessarily on a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my determination to succeed despite limited resources, the hours I spent studying on buses and during work breaks, or how I leveraged peer relationships to fill knowledge gaps. It also wouldn't capture the CBT session I did to manage my stress, or how surprised I was to actually achieve a distinction given everything I was juggling.
